videos.transcript:
So... What's the solution then? How can we have all this wonderful tool AI that we've heard so many talks about at Web Summit without losing control of AGI? 


I really like the very detailed narrow path plan that was put out by OpenAI recently. And here's my vision in summary. On the policy side, the US and China unilaterally decide to treat AI just like they treat any other powerful technology industry with binding safety standards. 


Not to appease the rival superpower, but just to prevent their own companies from causing harm and building uncontrollable AGI. 


Next, the US and China get together and push the rest of the world to join them in an AGI moratorium treaty so that AGI isn't built in North Korea or anywhere else. 


And after that, we get this amazing age of global prosperity fueled by amazing tool AI, like we're discussing here at Web Summit.
videos.title: Max Tegmark on Navigating the Path to Safe AGI
videos.viralReason: Max Tegmark discusses the path to safe tool AI, the risks of losing control to AGI, and the need for international safety standards between the US and China to prevent catastrophic outcomes.

videos.transcript:
Now, there are gloomers who argue that we can't have the kind of AI that you wanted without also getting the kind of AI that you don't want. 


But I'm going to argue that yes, we can. The kind of AI that you didn't vote for there in your show hands is AGI. Smarter than human AI that outperforms us at virtually all tasks, which includes tasks like building smarter robots that can build robot factories that can make more robots and could turn into a new digital species that we lose control over.
videos.title: Max Tegmark on the Existential Risks of AGI
videos.viralReason: Max Tegmark explicitly discusses the definition of AGI as 'smarter than human AI' that could turn into a 'new digital species that we lose control over', directly addressing existential risk and alignment concerns.

videos.transcript:
But you might say, It's surely controllable, so why don't you just shut up, Max, get off stage and stop worrying? 


Well, it is not controllable. We have no idea how to control it. And you don't have to take my word for it. Once these artificial intelligences get smarter than we are, they will take control. 


They'll make us irrelevant. And that's quite worrying. And nobody knows how to prevent that for sure.
videos.title: Why AGI is Fundamentally Uncontrollable
videos.viralReason: Tegmark explains why AGI is fundamentally uncontrollable, citing Alan Turing and the challenge of aligning goals between species of different intelligence levels.

videos.relatedTopic: ["Future of Life Institute","Existential risk","Elon Musk","AI Safety","Nerd research"]
videos.transcript:
You're a physics professor at MIT studying the cosmos, the nature of the cosmos, and you decide to start the Future of Life Institute to study research into the existential risk of AI. 


Why did you do that at that time, and also, How did you convince Elon Musk to donate $10 million to your organization? 


I decided to start that organization because I just felt humanity was falling so far short of our potential. 


I love technology. That's why I'm so happy I can work at a university that has technology in its name. And instead of building a great future with it, we're getting into these political and geopolitical pissing contests.
videos.title: Founding the Future of Life Institute for AI Safety
videos.viralReason: Max Tegmark details the founding of the Future of Life Institute and his collaboration with Elon Musk to fund research aimed at mitigating the existential risks of AI.

videos.transcript:
So it's for reasons like these that people like Sam Altman have said AGI could mean lights out for all of us. 


And last year, a who's who of AI experts said that this could cause human extinction. Now, I do want to commend the AGI companies for spending at least a small fraction of their money on trying to solve this unsolved control problem, but they're nowhere close, whatever their press releases say. 


The biggest success they've had so far is training large language models to not say harmful things, as opposed to making them not want harmful things.
videos.title: The Unsolved Control Problem and Human Extinction
videos.viralReason: The speaker addresses the 'unsolved control problem,' citing Sam Altman's 'lights out' comment and the risk of human extinction from agentic, self-improving systems.

videos.transcript:
As we describe in this paper here, we've had a revolution in AI's ability to produce art, to produce text, and soon code, and we're getting a revolution in AI's ability to produce proofs that the code does what you want it to do and meet your spec. 


So here's my technical nerd vision for this. You, the human, write down your specification that you want your tool to obey. 


Some very powerful neural network-based AI that you don't trust figures out how to make the tool, writes the code, writes the proof that it meets your spec, and wait a minute. 


How are you possibly going to understand this giant AI and the tool it made and the proof? Well, here's the great news. You don't need to understand any of that. 


Because just like it's much harder to find a needle in a haystack than to verify that it's a needle after you've found it, it's much harder to find a proof than to verify the proof after you've found it. 


And it can be done, in fact, with 300 lines of Python code on your laptop. And this is how we can not only make but also trust really powerful tool AI, even if it's made by systems we don't trust.
videos.title: Technical Solutions for Trustworthy Superintelligent AI
videos.viralReason: Tegmark explains a technical approach to AI safety using formal verification and proofs to ensure powerful AI systems meet human specifications without requiring trust.

videos.transcript:
Well, you would call bullshit on this because you know that if we have legally mandated safety standards, then companies will innovate and meet them and give you safe medicines. 


In the same way, if someone tells you, hey, we need to have completely unregulated AGI, otherwise you can't have any of the exciting tool AI that you're excited about here at Web Summit, you would call BS on that too. 


You should. Because if we have legally mandated safety standards saying that AI has to be controllable, companies will innovate to give you those tools.
videos.title: The Case for Mandated AI Safety Standards
videos.viralReason: This clip focuses on the necessity of legally mandated safety standards to ensure AI remains controllable, comparing it to biotech regulations, which is a core part of the AI safety and alignment debate.

videos.transcript:
I think the probability that it's physically possible to build it is basically 100%. What's powered the whole AI revolution is this insight that our brain is a biological computer. 


And it doesn't matter whether this information is being processed by carbon atoms in neurons and brains or by silicon atoms in our technology. 


Whether we'll actually build it will just depend on whether we, as a society, decide we want to or not. 


And as I'm arguing, there's absolutely no reason to try to rush and build it now or put ourselves in some weird situation where it's going to be built in two years and we just have to hope we can make it safe. 


That's so backward, right? We should set the safety standards there and then we can have all these wonderful AI tools.
videos.title: The 100% Probability of AGI and the Priority of Safety
videos.viralReason: The speaker quantifies the probability of building AGI and emphasizes that society must prioritize safety standards over the rush to build uncontrollable systems.

videos.transcript:
is not another country, but out-of-control AGI that will be way more powerful than any country has ever been. 


So I call this global... a battle to build AGI first, a hopium war, fueled by hopium, delusional hope that we can control AGI. 


Why? Because we're closer to building AGI than we are to figuring out how to align or control it. We're closer to building AGI than figuring out how to control it.
videos.title: The AGI Race: A Global Suicide Race
videos.viralReason: Tegmark frames the AGI race between US and China as a 'suicide race' because building AGI is moving faster than our ability to align or control it.

videos.transcript:
My theory of change with that was not that I thought we were going to immediately get a pause. My goal was just to make it socially safe for people to raise concerns. 


And that really worked remarkably. There were a lot of people who just had a lot of pent-up angst about this and were afraid of speaking up for fear of seeming like Luddites. 


And then after the pause letter and the extinction letter, nobody felt afraid of talking about this anymore when even Jeff Hinton and Joshua Bengio and others and the AI CEOs had signed these things. 


So that totally worked, and that in turn catalyzed all this political activity we're seeing now with international AI summits, AI safety institutes, and so on. 


But in terms of actually getting a pause, no, we certainly don't have one.
videos.title: How the AI Pause Letter Changed the Safety Conversation
videos.viralReason: Tegmark explains the impact of the AI pause letter in making it socially acceptable for top researchers to voice concerns about AI extinction risks.
